policy_statement: Seattle City Light will pilot Energy Efficiency as a Service (EEaS) as part of whole-building programs to enable deeper energy efficiency in commercial buildings.
mechanism_description: Pilot an EEaS program to overcome split incentives and enable commercial building efficiency upgrades, reducing building energy consumption.
primary_causal_pathway: Building upgrades → ↓ energy demand (same service) → ↓ fuel/electricity use → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Retrofit existing buildings by improving insulation/air sealing, upgrading HVAC and water heating equipment, optimizing controls, and/or installing efficient lighting and appliances.  
2) Mediating physical process: Improved thermal envelope and higher-efficiency end-use systems reduce heat transfer losses/gains and reduce energy required per unit of heating/cooling/lighting/service delivered.  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Lower onsite combustion (e.g., gas/oil) and reduced electricity demand reduce CO₂ (and some CH₄/N₂O) emissions from building operations and power generation.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced building energy consumption leading to lower upstream and onsite greenhouse gas emissions.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Retrofits reduce operational energy use, which directly reduces GHG emissions from energy supply—this is a mitigation pathway.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While some retrofits can improve heat resilience or comfort, the defining and largest climate effect of the mechanism “building_retrofit → energy_use_reduction” is emissions reduction via lower energy demand.  
3) Inherent causal chain for each secondary: None—adaptation (e.g., reduced heat stress during outages) or resource-efficiency benefits beyond energy are not universal to all retrofits.  
4) Edge cases applied: Retrofits that focus on passive survivability or weatherization still often reduce energy use, but if a policy’s intent were primarily hazard exposure reduction, that would be a different mechanism; here the mechanism explicitly centers energy-use reduction.  
5) Confirm labels apply to ALL instances: Any policy implementing this mechanism aims to cut building energy demand; reduced energy demand inherently drives emissions reductions, so “Mitigation” fits across contexts.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If a “retrofit” policy is specifically for resilience (e.g., floodproofing, backup power, cool refuges) without meaningful energy reduction, it should be classified under a different mechanism; similarly, deep retrofits paired with fuel-switching/electrification could introduce additional mitigation mechanisms beyond energy reduction, but that is not inherent to this mechanism as stated.
key_indicators: "pilot Energy Efficiency as a Service (EEaS)"; "whole-building programs"; "enable deeper energy efficiency"; "help participants overcome the split incentive barrier in commercial buildings"
co_benefits: Potential co-benefits: lower energy bills/operating costs for commercial buildings; potential improved indoor comfort and productivity.
instance_edge_case_notes: EEaS could be structured as on-bill financing or service-based contracting; without explicit details on pricing/repayment structure, it is best classified broadly as an incentive-program rather than a pricing-mechanism.
